nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef drop-off faces and deep pools inside reef crests; depth range 3–30 m, with most records 3–20 m. Endemic to Fiji, with occasional vagrant records from New Caledonia.

diet.notes: Primarily grazes on seaweeds and benthic algae in the wild (trophic level ~2.7). In captivity, generally takes dried marine algae (nori sheets), spirulina-based pellets and flakes, and a variety of fresh/blanched vegetables. Has been reported to occasionally accept meaty fare such as mysis or brine shrimp, but a vegetable-rich diet is what keeps the fish in good condition and reduces the chances of coral nipping.

avoidWith: very small slow-moving tankmates that may be intimidated at feeding time

summary: Hardy, peaceful herbivorous rabbitfish endemic to Fiji, with a striking dark-brown front and bright yellow rear. Venomous dorsal and anal spines; generally reef-safe when well fed.
careNotes: The Bicolor Foxface is a Fiji-endemic rabbitfish (occasional vagrants in New Caledonia) recognisable by its sharply two-toned body — a dark purplish-brown front two-thirds transitioning abruptly to a bright yellow caudal third, with yellow pectoral fins and soft dorsal/anal rays. Juveniles tend to form small schools, while adults are commonly observed in pairs along reef drop-offs, but in captivity the species is generally kept singly because of conspecific aggression with other rabbitfish. IMPORTANT: All dorsal and anal fin spines are venomous (similar in chemistry to stonefish toxin) and can deliver a painful sting; handle only with a net or aquarium tools, never with bare hands. Reef safety is conditional — a well-fed specimen typically ignores corals, but underfed fish have been reported to nip at soft corals and LPS polyps, so ensure ample algae is available (nori clips, seaweed sheets) and supplement with herbivore-formulated pellets. Like other rabbitfish, this species can adopt a mottled darker camouflage pattern when stressed, sleeping, or freshly introduced — this is normal and not a sign of illness. Tank-size sources disagree significantly: BlueZoo Aquatics lists 60 US gal (~227 L) while LiveAquaria recommends 130 US gal (~492 L); given the species' adult size (~24 cm), active swimming behaviour, and need for open space and rockwork territory, the larger recommendation of ~500 L is considered more appropriate for long-term care and is recorded here, but the wide spread is the reason dataStatus is set to needs_review. IUCN status is Near Threatened owing to its limited range and reef habitat degradation in Fiji.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is not documented for this species. Like other siganids, it is a pelagic broadcast spawner with planktonic larvae, and pairing/spawning cues are tied to lunar and tidal cycles — making reliable home-aquarium reproduction essentially infeasible. All specimens in the trade are wild-collected.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for husbandry parameters (temperature 72–78 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, max size 10 in, min tank 130 US gal, care level moderate, peaceful temperament, herbivorous diet, reef-safe with caution). Collection page at https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/foxface-rabbitfish redirected to the dedicated species product page.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked origin (Fiji), care level (intermediate), peaceful disposition, herbivorous diet, reef-safe with caution, max trade size ~15 in. Recommends min tank size 60 US gal (~227 L) — significantly lower than LiveAquaria's 130 US gal (~492 L); see careNotes for spread and reasoning behind the larger value recorded here.

additional.name: FishBase – Siganus uspi
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Siganidae, order Acanthuriformes), max length (24 cm TL), depth range (3–30 m, usually 3–20 m), endemic to Fiji with occasional New Caledonia records, environment (reef-associated, hard-coral drop-offs), diet (seaweeds, trophic level 2.7), preferred temperature 24–28 °C. Notes the species is venomous with stout, pungent spines. IUCN Near Threatened.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Siganus uspi
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum length (24 cm / 9.4 in), Fiji endemism, depth 3–20 m, behaviour (adults in pairs, juveniles in schools), coloration (dark purplish-brown front with yellow pectorals, soft dorsal/anal rays, caudal and rear body), venomous fin spines (chemistry similar to stonefish toxin), and IUCN Near Threatened status.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic broadcast spawner with planktonic larvae tied to lunar/tidal cycles; no reliable captive breeding documented.
